title: Procurement Planning
description: Learn how project managers determine what to buy, when to buy it, and how procurement decisions influence project success.

sections.title: Learning Objectives
items: Understand why procurement planning is important for project success.
Identify the key steps in the procurement planning process.
Understand how procurement planning supports project objectives.

sections.title: Introduction
content: Most projects require products, services or expertise that the project team cannot provide internally. These external resources must be obtained through procurement.
Procurement Planning is the process of deciding what needs to be purchased, when it should be purchased, from whom, and under what contractual conditions.
Good procurement planning ensures that external resources are available when needed while minimizing costs, delays and project risks.

sections.title: What is Procurement?
content: Procurement is the process of acquiring goods, services or works from external suppliers.
Examples include purchasing construction materials, hiring consultants, contracting software developers, renting equipment or outsourcing specialized engineering work.
Procurement differs from simply buying products because it involves planning, supplier selection, contract management and ongoing supplier relationships.

sections.title: Example: Building a Wind Farm
content: A company is constructing a new offshore wind farm.
The project requires turbines, underwater cables, installation vessels and specialist engineering services.

Since the project organization cannot produce these resources itself, it develops a procurement plan to determine when each supplier should be contracted and how deliveries will fit into the project schedule.

sections.title: Why Procurement Planning Matters
content: Many projects depend heavily on suppliers. Delays, poor quality or supplier failures can significantly impact project performance.
Effective procurement planning helps secure reliable suppliers, negotiate favorable contracts and coordinate deliveries with project activities.
Poor procurement planning often results in cost overruns, schedule delays and quality problems.

sections.title: The Make-or-Buy Decision
content: One of the first procurement decisions is determining whether work should be performed internally or outsourced to an external supplier.
This is known as the make-or-buy decision.

Organizations compare internal capabilities, available expertise, costs, available capacity, strategic priorities and project risks before deciding whether to perform the work themselves or purchase it externally.

sections.title: Example: Make or Buy
content: A software company needs cybersecurity expertise for a new project.
The company could train its own employees, but this would take several months.
Instead, management decides to hire an external cybersecurity consultant who can begin immediately, allowing the project to remain on schedule.

sections.title: The Procurement Planning Process
content: Procurement planning normally follows several steps.
First, the project team identifies which products or services must be obtained externally.
Next, procurement requirements are defined, including technical specifications, quality requirements, delivery schedules and budget limitations.
Potential suppliers are then identified and procurement strategies are developed before contracts are awarded.

sections.title: Selection Criteria
content: Choosing the lowest-priced supplier is not always the best decision.
Project managers evaluate suppliers using several criteria, including price, technical capability, quality, financial stability, experience, delivery performance and reputation.
The goal is to select suppliers that provide the best overall value rather than simply the lowest cost.

sections.title: Lowest Price Is Not Always Cheapest
content: A supplier with the lowest initial price may deliver poor quality, miss deadlines or require expensive rework.
These hidden costs can make the cheapest bid the most expensive option over the lifetime of the project.
Successful procurement focuses on total value rather than purchase price alone.

sections.title: Procurement Risks
content: Procurement introduces additional risks because project success depends on external organizations.
Common procurement risks include supplier bankruptcy, delivery delays, quality defects, price increases, contract disputes and geopolitical disruptions.
Project managers identify these risks during procurement planning and develop mitigation strategies before contracts are signed.

sections.title: Example: Shipping Industry
content: A ship management company plans a dry-docking project for one of its vessels.
Critical spare parts must arrive before the vessel enters the shipyard.
The procurement plan identifies approved suppliers, expected delivery times and backup suppliers in case delays occur, helping prevent costly off-hire periods.

sections.title: Expert Insight
content: Experienced project managers begin procurement planning early. Long lead-time items such as specialized equipment, vessels or industrial machinery often determine the entire project schedule. Delaying procurement planning can create bottlenecks that no amount of project crashing can eliminate.

sections.title: Common Mistakes
content: Waiting too long before contacting suppliers.
Selecting suppliers based only on the lowest price.
Writing unclear technical specifications.
Ignoring supplier risks.
Failing to coordinate procurement activities with the project schedule.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
items: Procurement planning determines what should be purchased, when, from whom and under which conditions.
The make-or-buy decision is one of the first procurement decisions.
Supplier selection should consider value, quality, capability and risk—not only price.
Good procurement planning reduces project risk and supports successful project delivery.
Procurement activities should always be integrated with the overall project schedule.

sections.title: Reflection
questions: When would outsourcing create more value than performing the work internally?
Why is the cheapest supplier not always the best supplier?
How could poor procurement planning delay an otherwise well-managed project?
